worth_it_explanation: The LinkedIn Audience Network (LAN) is enabled by default on most Sponsored Content ad sets, so a share of an account's spend leaves the LinkedIn feed without anyone deciding it should. These checks take a few minutes inside Campaign Manager. The Placements section shows whether the network is on and whether any publisher or category exclusions are in place, and the On/Off Network breakdown shows how the network is delivering against on-platform results. The fixes are configuration changes, not a rebuild, and reviewing the placement controls before relying on the network keeps ads off content the brand would not choose and keeps weak off-platform delivery from hiding inside a healthy average.
intro: The LinkedIn Audience Network extends Sponsored Content beyond the LinkedIn feed to ads on trusted third-party apps and websites. It is enabled by default on single image, carousel, document, and video ad sets, and it carries its own placement controls and its own delivery performance. These checks confirm that the network is enabled deliberately with the right brand-safety controls in place, and that its spend is read on its own rather than blended into the on-platform numbers.

audit_checks.title: The LinkedIn Audience Network is enabled without a placement or brand-safety review
audit_checks.impact_rationale: With the network on by default and no exclusions applied, ads can serve next to third-party content the brand would not choose, which is a brand-safety exposure rather than a reporting gap. Budget also leaves the LinkedIn feed for placements no one selected, so spend lands where it was never reviewed.

educate.base:
The LinkedIn Audience Network (LAN) serves an account's Sponsored Content ads on third-party apps and websites, off the LinkedIn feed. It is enabled by default for single image, carousel, document, and video ad sets, so an ad set inherits the network unless someone turns it off or shapes where it can run.

The placement controls that limit where LAN ads appear live in the ad set's Placements section and in the account's Brand Safety and Suitability Hub. They let an advertiser exclude publishers by Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) category, apply block or allow lists of specific sites and apps, and attach a third-party verification profile such as DoubleVerify. When none of these are set, the network places ads across its full inventory with no category or publisher restriction.

The risk is a placement next to content the brand would not choose, which a client may see before the auditor does. Because the network is on by default, this happens without anyone choosing it. You may want to confirm that the network is enabled on purpose and that the exclusions reflect the brand's standards, rather than treating the default-on state as a decision.

A few signs point to an unreviewed network setup:
  • No IAB category exclusions are applied at the account or ad set level
  • No publisher block list or allow list is in place
  • No third-party verification profile is attached to the ad sets running on the network
You may want to review the placement controls before relying on the network since the default leaves them empty.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a considered or business-to-business (B2B) store advertising on LinkedIn, the Audience Network can place product ads on third-party apps and sites where the brand has less control over the surrounding content. You may want to confirm the category exclusions and publisher lists match the standards the brand applies to its other placements.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, which is the common case on LinkedIn, the Audience Network extends lead ads to off-platform placements that the brand may not have reviewed. You may want to confirm that brand-safety exclusions are set before the network carries forms that represent the brand off-platform.

learn_more.source_title: Manage delivery preferences for the LinkedIn Audience Network
Enable or disable LinkedIn Audience Network for your Sponsored Content campaign

investigate.intro: Confirm in Campaign Manager whether the Audience Network is enabled on the ad sets and what placement and brand-safety controls limit where its ads run.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: Open Campaign Manager, select the account, and open a campaign group, then a campaign, then an ad set running Sponsored Content.
In the ad set's Placements section, note whether the LinkedIn Audience Network checkbox is enabled and whether any publisher block list, allow list, or category exclusions are applied.
Open the account's Brand Safety and Suitability Hub and note any IAB category exclusions, block or allow lists, and third-party verification profiles set at the account level.
Record, per ad set, whether the network is on and whether the brand-safety controls match the brand's standards or are left at the default empty state.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad set running Sponsored Content, whether the Audience Network is enabled and whether placement and brand-safety controls are in place. An ad set where the network is on with category exclusions, a publisher list, or a verification profile that reflect the brand's standards passes, as does an ad set where the network is deliberately off. An ad set running on the network with no exclusions and no verification profile is a fail recorded with the ad set name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Audience Network status and the brand-safety controls for each Sponsored Content ad set above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the LinkedIn Audience Network is enabled on {{site_name}}'s Sponsored Content ad sets without placement or brand-safety controls in place. The network is on by default and serves ads on third-party apps and sites, so without category exclusions, publisher lists, or a verification profile, the ads can run next to content the brand would not choose, and budget leaves the LinkedIn feed for placements no one reviewed. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each ad set's Placements section, deciding whether the network belongs in the plan, and applying IAB category exclusions, publisher block or allow lists, and a third-party verification profile that match the brand's standards. After the change, confirm the controls in the Brand Safety and Suitability Hub and watch where the network delivers.

audit_checks.title: Audience Network spend is not evaluated separately from on-platform delivery
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When off-platform Audience Network delivery is blended into one campaign number, weak network performance hides inside a healthy on-platform average. Decisions about budget and bidding then rest on a figure that mixes two delivery contexts, so spend can keep flowing to the network even when its own results are poor.

educate.base:
When the LinkedIn Audience Network (LAN) is enabled, an ad set delivers in two places, the LinkedIn feed and third-party apps and sites. By default the Campaign Manager reporting dashboard reports the two together in a single set of numbers, so clicks, impressions, and conversions from the network sit inside the same totals as on-platform delivery.

Off-platform delivery often performs differently from delivery in the feed, in cost per result, in completion rates, and in conversion rate. Read as one blended figure, a weak network result can sit hidden inside a healthy on-platform average, and the campaign looks fine while a portion of its spend underperforms.

Campaign Manager can separate the two. The reporting dashboard offers an On/Off Network breakdown that splits results into On LinkedIn and Audience Network, and the same split can be exported to a CSV for a closer look. One detail to keep in mind is that conversions from Audience Network placements are reported at the ad set level only, so the breakdown is read per ad set rather than per ad.

You may want to apply the On/Off Network breakdown to any ad set running on the network and judge the network's delivery on its own. If its cost per result or conversion rate trails the feed by a wide margin, that is a budget and placement decision the blended number hides.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a considered or business-to-business (B2B) store, off-platform Audience Network delivery can carry a different conversion rate than the LinkedIn feed, so a blended figure can make the network look like it is pulling its weight when it is not. You may want to read the On/Off Network breakdown before deciding how much budget the network should carry.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, the common case on LinkedIn, the cost per lead from Audience Network placements can differ from the feed. You may want to break out the network's lead performance so a higher off-platform cost per lead does not hide inside the campaign average.

learn_more.source_title: Performance reporting for LinkedIn Audience Network on/off network breakdown

investigate.intro: Confirm in Campaign Manager whether Audience Network delivery is being read separately from on-platform delivery using the On/Off Network breakdown.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: Open Campaign Manager, select the account, and open the reporting dashboard for a campaign whose ad sets run on the Audience Network.
Select the Breakdown control at the top of the dashboard and choose On/Off Network to split results into On LinkedIn and Audience Network.
Compare the cost per result, conversion rate, and any quality metrics between the two contexts, reading conversions at the ad set level since Audience Network conversions report there only.
If a closer look is needed, select Export and choose the Audience network ad performance report to download the split as a CSV.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign with ad sets on the Audience Network, whether the On/Off Network breakdown has been read and how network delivery compares with on-platform delivery. A campaign where the network split has been reviewed and its results are acceptable or acted on passes. A campaign where the network and feed are read only as one blended number is a fail recorded with the campaign name, and a campaign where the network trails the feed by a wide margin with no action is a fail noting the gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the On/Off Network breakdown for each campaign running on the Audience Network above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Audience Network delivery is read together with on-platform delivery rather than on its own. Off-platform results often differ from the LinkedIn feed, so blended into a single number, weak network performance can sit hidden inside a healthy on-platform average, and budget decisions rest on a figure that mixes the two. {{voice_recommend}} applying the On/Off Network breakdown in Campaign Manager to every campaign running on the network, comparing the network's cost per result and conversion rate against the feed, and reading conversions at the ad set level where the network reports them. After the review, decide whether the network's own delivery justifies its share of the budget.
